license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: The operating system started at system time
entries.details: Generated by the kernel on system startup. Records the time Windows started. Useful for establishing a system timeline and correlating with unexpected reboots or gaps in log coverage.

investigationPivots: An EID 12 not preceded by EID 13 indicates an unexpected reboot — correlate with EID 41 and 6008 for root cause
Unexpected reboots during an active incident may indicate attacker-initiated restarts to apply persistence or clear memory
Use startup timestamps to identify gaps in log coverage that may represent evidence destruction windows
commonFalsePositives: Normal system startups after planned reboots or shutdowns
Windows Update-initiated reboots

entries.title: The operating system is shutting down at system time
entries.details: Generated by the kernel on clean shutdown. Absence of this event between two EID 12 (startup) events indicates an unexpected shutdown, crash, or forced reboot.

investigationPivots: Absence of EID 13 between two EID 12 events is the key signal — this pattern indicates an unclean shutdown
Correlate shutdown time with user logon activity (Security EID 4634/4647) to determine if a user was active at the time
commonFalsePositives: Normal planned shutdowns initiated by users or IT

entries.title: The system has rebooted without cleanly shutting down first
entries.details: Generated by Kernel-Power after an unexpected reset, BSOD, or power loss. Correlate with 6008 for pre-OS context and examine minidump files for root cause. May also be generated after a forced hard reboot by an attacker.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 6008 for the previous shutdown timestamp to establish when the unexpected reboot occurred
Check %SystemRoot%\Minidump for crash dump files — kernel-mode crashes (BSOD) leave minidumps that can identify the faulting driver or code
An attacker-initiated hard reboot (e.g., via shutdown /r /f) generates this event — correlate with Security EID 4688 for shutdown.exe or equivalent
commonFalsePositives: Power loss or hardware failure
Kernel panic caused by a buggy driver or faulty hardware

entries.title: The System log file was cleared
entries.details: Generated when the System event log is cleared. Indicator of potential evidence destruction or log tampering. Correlate with Security 1102 which fires when the Security log is cleared.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs

investigationPivots: Identify who cleared the log via concurrent Security EID 4624 and 4688 events around the same timestamp
Correlate with Security EID 1102 — simultaneous clearing of both logs is a strong evidence destruction indicator
Log clearing near the end of a timeline gap may indicate the attacker was covering tracks before departing
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators clearing logs for maintenance (rare, and should be documented in change records)

entries.title: System shutdown or restart initiated by process
entries.summary: A process or user initiated a shutdown or restart.
entries.details: Generated when a process calls a shutdown or restart API (ExitWindowsEx, InitiateSystemShutdown). Key fields: the initiating process (ImagePath), the user context, the reason code, and the shutdown type (shutdown vs. restart). This event captures intentional, process-initiated power actions — unlike EID 41 and 6008 which reflect unclean shutdowns. An attacker may initiate a restart to apply a new service or driver, clear memory artefacts, or force re-authentication. Shutdown reasons citing 'Other (Unplanned)' or no reason string, initiated by unexpected processes, warrant investigation.

investigationPivots: ImagePath identifies the initiating process — shutdowns from cmd.exe, powershell.exe, or unknown processes outside System32 are suspicious
Reason code 'Other (Unplanned)' with no comment string may indicate an attacker-initiated reboot
Correlate with Security EID 4688 to confirm the process ancestry of the initiating binary
commonFalsePositives: Scheduled reboots initiated by Windows Update (wuauclt.exe, TrustedInstaller.exe)
User-initiated shutdowns or restarts from the Start menu (initiated by explorer.exe)

entries.title: The Event Log service was started
entries.summary: Windows Event Log service started (system boot marker).
entries.details: Generated by the Event Log service when it starts, which occurs during system boot. Because this event is written before most other services initialize, its timestamp is commonly used as a precise system boot time marker. An EID 6005 with no preceding EID 6006 from the prior session indicates an unclean shutdown — pair with EID 6008 and EID 41 to investigate. Gaps between EID 6006 (service stopped) and the next EID 6005 represent the downtime window and should be correlated with expected maintenance schedules.

investigationPivots: Use EID 6005 timestamps to establish precise boot times for timeline analysis
Absence of a preceding EID 6006 indicates the prior session ended uncleanly — correlate with EID 41 and 6008
Unexpected boot times (e.g., middle of the night with no planned maintenance) warrant investigation
commonFalsePositives: Normal system boot after any planned or unplanned restart

entries.title: The Event Log service was stopped
entries.summary: Windows Event Log service stopped (clean shutdown marker).
entries.details: Generated when the Event Log service stops, which occurs during a clean system shutdown. This is one of the last events written to the System log before a clean shutdown completes. An EID 6005 not preceded by EID 6006 (from the prior session) indicates an unclean shutdown — the Event Log service did not have time to write this record. Correlate EID 6006 with EID 13 (kernel shutdown) to confirm a clean shutdown sequence.

investigationPivots: Presence of EID 6006 confirms a clean shutdown occurred — absence before the next EID 6005 indicates an unclean restart
Correlate with EID 1074 to identify what process initiated the shutdown that produced this event
Gap between EID 6006 and the next EID 6005 represents the system's downtime window
commonFalsePositives: Normal clean shutdown after any planned restart or power-off

entries.title: The previous system shutdown was unexpected
entries.details: Indicates crash, forced reboot, or power loss. The event records the date and time of the unexpected shutdown. Correlate with EID 41 which fires after the subsequent reboot.

investigationPivots: The timestamp in this event indicates when the previous unexpected shutdown occurred — use it to anchor your investigation timeline
Correlate with EID 41 on the same boot to cross-validate the crash timing
An unexpected shutdown immediately following a period of suspicious activity is a strong indicator of attacker-initiated reboot
commonFalsePositives: Power outage or UPS failure
Hardware failure causing unexpected system halt

entries.title: System uptime
entries.summary: System uptime recorded (logged daily at midnight).
entries.details: Generated daily at midnight by the Event Log service, recording the system's total uptime in seconds. While primarily a diagnostic event, EID 6013 is a useful timeline anchor. If the uptime reported in EID 6013 is inconsistent with the expected time since the last EID 6005 (boot), it may indicate a system time change (correlate with Security EID 4616). Absence of expected 6013 entries may indicate log tampering or a system that was not running during those periods.

investigationPivots: Use the uptime value to cross-validate the boot time derived from EID 6005 or EID 12
Missing expected 6013 entries (e.g., no entry for a day the system should have been online) may indicate log gaps or tampering
Uptime inconsistent with the last known boot time may indicate a clock change — correlate with Security EID 4616
commonFalsePositives: Any online system will generate this event daily — high volume is expected

entries.title: A service failed to start
entries.summary: Service Control Manager failed to start a service.
entries.details: Generated when Service Control Manager fails to start a service. Key fields: ServiceName and the error code. Common causes include a missing executable, access denied on the binary, or a dependency failure. From a DFIR perspective, a malicious service registered for persistence (EID 7045) but whose payload has been removed or blocked will generate this event on the next boot. A security tool removing the malicious binary while leaving the service registration intact is a common pattern — EID 7000 on boot for an unknown service is worth investigating.

investigationPivots: Correlate ServiceName with EID 7045 to determine when the service was installed and review its ImagePath
Error 2 (file not found) on a recently installed service may indicate the payload was removed by a security tool
Recurring EID 7000 on boot for the same service indicates a persistent failed service registration that should be investigated
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate services that fail to start due to dependency issues or misconfiguration after OS updates
Trial software leaving service registrations behind after uninstallation

entries.title: A service depends on another service that failed to start
entries.summary: Service failed because a dependency service did not start.
entries.details: Generated when a service fails to start because one of its dependencies did not start. Key fields: the failing service and the dependency service it requires. Cascading service failures can sometimes reveal an attacker disrupting a critical service (the dependency) to prevent a security tool from starting. For example, if an EDR agent depends on a driver service and that driver fails, the EDR will generate EID 7001. Investigate the root cause of the dependency failure (EID 7000 for the dependency) to determine whether it was deliberate.

investigationPivots: Identify the dependency service that failed — trace it back with EID 7000 to find the root failure
If the dependency is a security product driver or agent, investigate whether it was deliberately disabled or its binary tampered with
Cascading failures starting from an unusual or recently installed service warrant a full service dependency chain review
commonFalsePositives: Network-dependent services failing to start before network initialization completes on boot
Services with optional dependencies that are not installed on all configurations

entries.title: A service terminated with an error
entries.details: Generated when a service terminates with a non-zero error code. Useful for detecting security product failures or misconfigured services. Pivot on service name and error code to diagnose root cause.

investigationPivots: Service name is the primary pivot — errors in security products (AV, EDR, audit log service) warrant immediate investigation
Error code can indicate access denied (0x5), which may mean an attacker modified permissions to prevent restart
Correlate with EID 7036 to confirm whether the service entered the stopped state
commonFalsePositives: Misconfigured third-party services terminating due to dependency or configuration errors

entries.title: A service terminated unexpectedly
entries.details: Generated when a service terminates unexpectedly and a recovery action is configured. Records the number of times the service has crashed and the corrective action taken. Critical for detecting failures in security products or EDR agents.

investigationPivots: Crash count field — a high or incrementing count may indicate an attacker repeatedly killing a service that auto-restarts
Corrective action field — 'Restart the service' means the service came back; 'Run a program' may reveal a recovery command
Correlate with Security EID 4688 to identify processes that may have terminated the service
commonFalsePositives: Buggy or resource-starved third-party services entering crash loops
Services crashing due to OS patching or dependency changes

entries.title: A service crashed unexpectedly
entries.details: Generated when a service terminates unexpectedly and no recovery action is configured. Records the crash count. Similar to 7031 but without a configured recovery response.

investigationPivots: No recovery action means the service stays stopped — if it is a security product, the system may be unprotected
Crash count distinguishes a one-time failure from a repeating pattern — repeated crashes without recovery may indicate deliberate termination
Correlate with Security EID 4688 around the same timestamp to check for suspicious process activity
commonFalsePositives: Services that crash due to bugs and are not configured with recovery actions

entries.title: The service entered the running/stopped state
entries.details: Generated when a service enters the running or stopped state. High-volume informational event. Pivot on service name to detect unexpected service starts, stops, or the disabling of security tooling.

investigationPivots: Filter on security-relevant service names (e.g., WinDefend, Sense, EventLog, wuauserv) — unexpected stops are high priority
Correlate unexpected service stops with Security EID 4688 to identify what process triggered the state change
A service entering stopped state without a preceding EID 7023/7031/7034 may indicate a graceful but unauthorized stop
commonFalsePositives: Normal service state changes during Windows Update, patch application, or system maintenance

entries.title: The start type of a service was changed
entries.details: Generated when a service's start type is changed (e.g., from Disabled to Automatic). Changing a service start type to auto-start or boot-start is a persistence indicator. Correlate with 7045 for new service installations.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

investigationPivots: The old and new start type values are the key pivot — a change from Disabled or Manual to Automatic or Boot Start is a persistence indicator
Changes to defensive service start types (e.g., disabling WinDefend auto-start) are defense evasion
Correlate with Security EID 4688 to identify the process that made the change
commonFalsePositives: Software installers configuring their service start types during installation
Group Policy applying service configuration changes

entries.title: A service was installed in the system
entries.details: Generated when a new service is installed on the system. Key fields: ServiceName, ImagePath (the executable path), ServiceType, and StartType. ImagePath pointing outside of System32 or known application directories, or containing encoded commands or scripting engines, is a strong persistence indicator. Correlate with Security EID 4697 which captures the same event in the Security log.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

investigationPivots: ImagePath is the primary pivot — flag paths outside System32 or known application directories, especially user-writable locations or temp directories
ImagePath containing cmd.exe, powershell.exe, or scripting engines indicates a script-based service (common attacker technique)
Correlate with Security EID 4697 to identify the account that installed the service
StartType of Boot Start or System Start means the service runs before most security tooling
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software installing services during setup
Windows Update and driver installations adding new services
